items.title: Transfer $73,000 within Police budget for injury settlement costs
items.summary: City Manager Yi-An Huang requested transferring $73,000 from the Police Salary and Wages account to the Police Travel and Training (Judgment and Damages) account to cover settlement costs for police personnel injured on duty. Order adopted unanimously 9-0-0.
items.body: A communication transmitted from Yi-An Huang, City Manager, relative to the transfer of $73,000.00 from the General Fund Police Salary and Wages account to the General Fund Police Travel and Training (Judgment and Damages) account to cover current and anticipated settlement costs for police personnel injured in the performance of their duties.

vote.result: Order Adopted and CMA Placed on File [9-0-0]

items.title: Appropriate $37,411.95 Shannon grant for hot-spot patrols, youth violence reduction, and Community Art Center teen program
items.summary: City Manager Yi-An Huang requested appropriating $37,411.95 from the Metropolitan Mayors Coalition's Community Safety Initiative Shannon grant to fund hot spot patrols, youth violence reduction strategies, the Focused Deterrence program, and the Teen Public Art "Upcycling and Entrepreneurialism" program through the Community Art Center. Order adopted 8-0-1, with Councillor Simmons absent.
items.body: A communication transmitted from Yi-An Huang, City Manager, relative to the appropriation of $37,411.95 from the Metropolitan Mayors Coalition's Community Safety Initiative Shannon grant to support hot spot patrols, youth violence reduction strategies, and the Focused Deterrence program, as well as the Teen Public Art program "Upcycling and Entrepreneurialism" through the Community Art Center.

items.title: Urge Harvard to end time caps for non-tenure-track teaching faculty and recognize researchers' union rights
items.summary: A policy order putting the Council on record urging Harvard to end the practice of time caps for non-tenure-track teaching faculty and to acknowledge employee status of all researchers, including their right to union representation regardless of funding source. Adopted as Amended 8-0-1-0, with Councillor Zusy voting Present.
items.body: That the City Council go on the record urging Harvard administration to end the practice of time caps for non-tenure track teaching faculty and urging Harvard administration to acknowledge the labor contribution and employee status of all its researchers, regardless of funding source, and contractually recognize these researchers' protected right to union representation.
amendments: Adopted as Amended (text amendments not detailed in Final Actions)

rationale.work.audience:cambridge_workers: non-tenure-track faculty and researchers working in Cambridge

items.title: Halt design and construction work on Garden Street two-way traffic reconfiguration
items.summary: A policy order asking the City Manager to direct the Cambridge Department of Transportation (CDOT) and other relevant departments to halt all further design, engineering, procurement, or construction work on the proposed reconfiguration of Garden Street to restore two-way motor-vehicle traffic. Charter Right exercised by Councillor Flaherty; no final vote at this meeting.
items.body: That the City Manager is requested to direct the Cambridge Department of Transportation (CDOT) and any other relevant departments to halt any further design, engineering, procurement, or construction activities related to implementing a reconfiguration of Garden Street to restore two-way motor-vehicle traffic. CHARTER RIGHT EXERCISED BY COUNCILLOR FLAHERTY IN COUNCIL APRIL 13, 2026

vote.result: Charter Right Exercised by Councillor Flaherty

history.action: Charter Right Exercised by Councillor Flaherty

rationale.doc.status:charter_right_exercised: RESULT: Charter Right Exercised by Councillor Flaherty
rationale.geo.neighborhood:neighborhood_nine: Garden Street runs through Neighborhood Nine
rationale.transport.mode:bike: Garden Street reconfiguration is tied to a separated bike lane
rationale.transport.subject:bike_lane: two-way motor traffic restoration affects existing bike-lane configuration

items.title: Report on artificial-turf decision at Ahern Field; pause construction until report delivered
items.summary: A policy order asking the City Manager to report on how the decision to install artificial turf at Ahern Field was made, the rationale, and how community and public-health concerns were addressed, and to ensure construction does not move forward until the report is delivered. Adopted as Amended unanimously.
items.body: That the City Manager is requested work with relevant City departments to provide a report on how the decision to install artificial turf at Ahern Field was made, the rationale for artificial turf, and what process was taken to ensure community concerns and public health considerations were fully addressed, and to ensure that construction will not move forward until a report is delivered.
amendments: Adopted as Amended; additional sponsors added in Final Actions (Zusy, Flaherty, Vice Mayor Azeem, Al-Zubi)

rationale.doc.sponsor:zusy: COUNCILLOR ZUSY added in Final Actions
rationale.doc.sponsor:flaherty: COUNCILLOR FLAHERTY added in Final Actions

rationale.doc.sponsor:al_zubi: COUNCILLOR AL-ZUBI added in Final Actions

rationale.topic.area:public_health: public health considerations cited in body
rationale.topic.area:climate: artificial turf is a climate / environmental concern

items.summary: A policy order asking the City Manager to direct the Community Development Department to prepare a draft zoning petition strengthening Active Use requirements on Massachusetts Avenue, applying Option 2 from the Cambridge Street recommendations while keeping Warren Street in the CAM-6 District. Adopted unanimously 9-0-0.
items.body: That the City Manager is requested to direct the Community Development Department and relevant staff to prepare a draft zoning petition to strengthen Active Use requirements on Massachusetts Avenue per the recommendations and Option 2 on Cambridge Street, while keeping Warren Street in the CAM-6 District.

items.title: Chapter 2.129 Welcoming Community Ordinance — Unfinished Business, eligible to be ordained April 20, 2026
items.summary: Chapter 2.129 Welcoming Community Ordinance carried forward as Unfinished Business. It was passed to a second reading at the March 30, 2026 meeting and is eligible to be ordained on April 20, 2026. No vote was taken on the ordinance at this meeting.
items.body: Chapter 2.129 Welcoming Community Ordinance. PASSED TO A SECOND READING IN COUNCIL MARCH 30, 2026. ELIGIBLE TO BE ORDAINED APRIL 20, 2026.

history.action: Passed to a Second Reading in Council
Carried as Unfinished Business; eligible to be ordained April 20, 2026

rationale.topic.area:immigration: Welcoming Community Ordinance addresses immigrant residents

items.title: Awaiting Report: Raise residential parking permit fee to $75 with $25 low-income subsidy; remove senior exemption; cap at 2 cars/household
items.summary: Awaiting Report tracking a request for the City Manager to raise the residential parking permit fee to $75, create a self-identified $25 subsidized fee for residents in affordable housing, on SNAP, or low income, remove the senior exemption, and lower the per-resident permit cap from four to two.
items.body: That the City Manager is requested to work with relevant City departments to raise the fee of the parking permit program for all residents to $75, consider how to include a self-identified check off option so as not to increase administrative costs for a subsidized fee of $25 for residents who live in affordable housing, are enrolled in a program such as SNAP or are low income, remove the senior exemption for the residential parking permit program and lower the number of cars that individual residents are allowed to get a residential parking permit for from four to two. Councillor Nolan, Councillor Zusy, Councillor Sobrinho-Wheeler, Councillor McGovern

rationale.geo.scope:citywide: residential parking permit program is citywide
rationale.housing.tenure:any: permits affect both renter and owner residents
rationale.housing.affordability:inclusionary: subsidized fee for residents who live in affordable housing
rationale.transport.mode:drive: residential parking permit program affects drivers
rationale.fiscal.kind:parking_fee: raise the fee of the parking permit program
rationale.demo.age:senior_65plus: remove the senior exemption for the residential parking permit program
rationale.demo.income:low: subsidized fee of $25 for residents who ... are low income
rationale.demo.income:means_tested: enrolled in a program such as SNAP

items.title: Awaiting Report: open city-owned parking lots (esp. Tobin/Darby Vassal) for after-hours resident use
items.summary: Awaiting Report tracking a request for the City Manager to work with the School Department, Public Works, and other departments to make city-owned parking lots — focusing on school complexes including the under-construction Tobin/Darby Vassal lot — available for after-hours resident use. (Carryover from AR-25-29.)
items.body: That the City Manager is requested to work with the School Department, the Department of Public Works, and other relevant departments to ensure that all city owned parking lots, with a focus on school complexes, including the still under construction parking at Tobin/Darby Vassal school complex, could be made available for after-hours use by residents. Original Awaiting Report Item AR-25-29. Councillor Nolan

Original Awaiting Report Item AR-25-51. Councillor Nolan, Councillor Sobrinho-Wheeler, Councillor McGovern, Mayor Siddiqui

items.title: Awaiting Report: comprehensive review of safety/security at Cambridge City Hall
items.summary: Awaiting Report tracking a request for the City Manager to conduct a comprehensive review of existing safety and security measures at Cambridge City Hall, covering physical access controls, visitor screening, on-site security staffing and training, emergency response and evacuation, and surveillance/alarm technologies.
items.body: That the City Manager conduct a comprehensive review of existing safety and security measures at Cambridge City Hall, including an assessment of physical access controls, visitor screening practices, on-site security staffing and training, emergency response and evacuation protocols, and the use of security technologies such as surveillance systems and alarm monitoring. Councillor Flaherty, Councillor Simmons

items.title: Awaiting Report: review digital-equity work and update evaluation of internet access programs by June 2026
items.summary: Awaiting Report tracking a request for the City Manager to direct departments to review the city's digital equity work to date, update the evaluation of existing internet access programs, propose how to better meet residents' digital needs, and report back to the Council by June 2026.
items.body: The City Manager is requested to direct the appropriate departments to review the city's digital equity work to date, including the study's recommendations and all steps taken since the study conclusion in order to update the evaluation of existing internet access programs, assessing whether residents' digital needs are or could be better provided for and to propose how we can better meet their needs and to report back to the Council by June 2026 on internet access programs. Councillor Zusy, Councillor Nolan, Councillor Sobrinho-Wheeler, Vice Mayor Azeem

rationale.doc.sponsor:azeem: Vice Mayor Azeem listed as sponsor
rationale.demo.income:low: digital-equity programs primarily target lower-income residents lacking access

items.title: Awaiting Report: feasibility of permitting third-party guarantors/co-signers for Inclusionary Housing applicants
items.summary: Awaiting Report tracking a request for the City Manager to direct CDD to examine the feasibility, legal considerations, and administrative requirements of permitting qualified third-party guarantors or co-signers for applicants to Inclusionary Housing units, and to recommend whether this could responsibly expand access.
items.body: The City Manager is requested to direct the Community Development Department (CDD) to examine the feasibility, legal considerations, and administrative requirements of permitting qualified third-party guarantors or co-signers for applicants to Inclusionary Housing units and provide recommendations as to whether such a policy could responsibly expand access to these units. Vice Mayor Azeem, Councillor McGovern, Councillor Sobrinho-Wheeler, Councillor Simmons

items.title: Awaiting Report: simplify processes for paying election workers (IRS guidance review)
items.summary: Awaiting Report tracking a request for the City Manager to work with City departments and Election Commissioners to review IRS guidance and consult with other municipalities to simplify the process for paying election workers.
items.body: That the City Manager is requested to work with relevant City departments as well as the Election Commissioners to review guidance from the IRS and consult with other municipalities in order to simplify our processes for paying election workers. items.title: Ordinance Committee report — Cambridge St / Mass Ave active-use zoning hearing (March 11, 2026)
items.summary: The Ordinance Committee held a public hearing on March 11, 2026 on zoning recommendations to strengthen active-use requirements on Cambridge Street and Mass Ave, as a follow-up to the recently adopted zoning petitions from the Our Cambridge Street Planning Study and the Mass Ave Planning Study. Report Accepted and Placed on File by voice vote (VV9).
items.body: The Ordinance Committee held a public hearing on March 11, 2026 to have a conversation regarding zoning recommendations to strengthen active use requirements on Cambridge Street and Mass Ave as a follow-up to the recently adopted zoning petitions following the Our Cambridge Street Planning Study and Mass Ave Planning Study.

vote.result: Report Accepted and Placed on File [VV9]

history.action: Report Accepted and Placed on File [VV9]

rationale.doc.status:report_accepted: RESULT: Report Accepted and Placed on File [VV9]

items.title: Ordinance Committee report — April 6 follow-up hearing recommending formula-business permits, Mass Ave Active Use draft, O'Brien Highway rezoning
items.summary: The Ordinance Committee held a follow-up public hearing on April 6, 2026 and recommended three actions to the full Council: (1) require special permits for formula businesses on Cambridge Street; (2) draft a zoning petition strengthening Active Use requirements on Mass Ave per Option 2 (Warren Street stays in CAM-6); (3) draft language to rezone a section of O'Brien Highway to B.A. Zoning District. Report Accepted and Placed on File by voice vote (VV9).
items.body: The Ordinance Committee held a public hearing on April 6, 2026 to continue the discussion that was held at the March 11, 2026 hearing regarding zoning recommendations to strengthen active use requirements on Cambridge Street and Mass Ave as a follow-up on the recently adopted zoning petitions. 1. That the Ordinance Committee recommend to the full City Council that the City Manager direct the Community Development Department and relevant staff to include a requirement for special permits for formula businesses on Cambridge Street. 2. That the Ordinance Committee recommend to the full City Council that the City Manager direct the Community Development Department and relevant staff prepare a draft zoning petition to strengthen Active Use requirements on Massachusetts Avenue per the recommendations and Option 2 on Cambridge Street, while keeping Warren Street in the CAM-6 District. 3. That the Ordinance Committee recommend to the full City Council that the City Manager direct the Community Development Department to create draft language to propose changing the section of O'Brien Highway to B.A. Zoning District.

vote.result: Report Accepted and Placed on File [VV9]

history.action: Report Accepted and Placed on File [VV9]
